Glossary About
East Asian Traditions

Dōgen

1200 – 1253

Dōgen Kigen, or Dōgen Zenji, 1200–1253, is not a “philosopher of mind” in the modern disciplinary sense.

Dōgen Kigen, or Dōgen Zenji, 1200–1253, is not a “philosopher of mind” in the modern disciplinary sense. He did not write a treatise on consciousness, intentionality, qualia, cognition, perception, selfhood, or the mind-body problem as those terms appear in later European and analytic traditions. Yet his work contains one of the most powerful premodern accounts of mind when “mind” is understood not as a private inner substance but as the dynamic, embodied, temporal, self-world activity through which delusion and awakening are enacted. The most defensible reconstruction is this: Dōgen offers a nondual, non-substantialist, practice-centered, body-mind-world process account of experience. Mind is not a thing inside the head, not an immaterial soul, not a merely representational faculty, and not a permanent witness behind experience. Mind is the lived, enacted, temporally unfolding activity of body, world, practice, language, and things themselves.

Historically, Dōgen stands at a distinctive intersection. He inherits Indian Buddhist no-self analysis, East Asian Mahāyāna emptiness and Buddha-nature thought, Chinese Chan practice, and Japanese Kamakura-era religious reform. He also develops something philosophically original: an account in which practice and realization are not means and end, but one event. His mature view makes mental life intelligible only as lived praxis. The question is therefore not “What is the mind as an object?” but “How is body-mind-world actualized, moment by moment, as delusion or awakening?” Dōgen’s early training in Tendai Buddhism, his journey to China, his study under Tiantong Rujing in the Caodong lineage, and his later inauguration of what became Japanese Sōtō Zen all matter for interpreting this point. He returned to Japan in 1227, wrote works including Fukan zazengi, Bendōwa, and the many-fascicle Shōbōgenzō, and repeatedly insisted that zazen and everyday activity are not subordinate preliminaries to enlightenment but the very field in which enlightenment is realized.

1. Methodological orientation: how to extract a “philosophy of mind” from Dōgen

A rigorous extraction has to avoid two opposite errors. The first error is antiquarian: treating Dōgen only as a religious author, poet, monastic reformer, or Zen master, and therefore refusing to read him philosophically. The second error is assimilationist: translating him too quickly into Cartesian, phenomenological, analytic, or cognitive-scientific terms and thereby losing what is most distinctive about him. Dōgen’s thought is philosophical, but it is philosophical in a mode where ontology, phenomenology, ethics, language, practice, and soteriology are inseparable.

Modern philosophy of mind often begins from questions such as: What is consciousness? How do mental states represent objects? How does the mind relate to the body? What is the self? What is agency? How can subjective experience fit into the natural world? Dōgen does not answer those questions by first positing a subject and then asking how it contacts an external object. He challenges the very grammar that makes the subject-object divide seem basic. His characteristic move is to show that what we call “self,” “mind,” “body,” “practice,” “time,” and “world” are not independent substances but mutually actualizing events.

This means that a Dōgenian philosophy of mind should not be reconstructed as a doctrine of “consciousness” alone. It should be reconstructed across several connected axes: no-self, body-mind, nonthinking, perception, temporality, agency, language, and practice-realization. The mind is not merely observed; it is transformed by how one sits, walks, cooks, washes, speaks, bows, studies, and encounters things. Dōgen’s philosophy of mind is therefore also a philosophy of training.

2. Buddhist background: no-self, aggregates, dependent arising, and cognition

Dōgen’s account of mind emerges from a Buddhist intellectual world in which the idea of a permanent, independent, self-identical subject had already been subjected to sustained critique. Indian Buddhist philosophy developed detailed analyses of mind over many centuries, but those analyses were normally framed by the doctrine of no-self: mental phenomena are not grounded in a substantial, autonomous, enduring ego. Instead, personal existence is analyzed in terms of impermanent aggregates, dependent conditions, causal processes, and streams of cognition.

This matters because Dōgen does not arrive at nondual body-mind thought by rejecting a Cartesian dualism that had first been dominant in his own world. Rather, he inherits a tradition in which the self had already been decomposed into impermanent processes. Buddhist Abhidharma traditions treated cognitions as intentional, that is, as object-involving episodes, while also debating the relation between momentary mental events, continuity, memory, and selfhood.

Mahāyāna developments add further dimensions. Madhyamaka emphasizes emptiness: things lack independent, inherent self-nature because they arise dependently. Yogācāra develops sophisticated analyses of consciousness, but it is misleading to treat it as simple Western-style idealism; responsible accounts emphasize that Yogācāra does not posit an individual or universal mental substance in the manner of Berkeleyan or Hegelian idealism.

Dōgen’s own philosophy should be read against this background. He is not saying “only mind exists” in the sense of a mental substance replacing a material substance. Nor is he saying “the self is unreal” in the crude sense that nothing matters or no one acts. He radicalizes a Buddhist process ontology: self, mind, body, action, world, and time are all dependently actualized. The self is not an entity behind experience; it is a pattern of enacted relation. The problem is not that we have experience; the problem is that we reify experience into a subject over here and objects over there.

3. Dōgen’s historical position in Japanese Buddhism

Dōgen belongs to the early Kamakura period, an era of major social and religious transformation in Japan. He began in the Tendai environment on Mt. Hiei but became dissatisfied with inherited explanations of practice and realization. The standard problem attributed to his early quest is: if all beings already have Buddha-nature, why is arduous practice necessary? His eventual answer is not that practice produces enlightenment as a later reward. It is that practice and realization are one. Practice is not an instrumental bridge to awakening; practice is awakening enacted.

This answer places him in both continuity and tension with Zen. He inherits Chan/Sōtō emphasis on zazen, direct practice, and realization, but he is sharply critical of shallow anti-intellectualism, quietism, and any doctrine that treats enlightenment as either a private inner state or a possession one can acquire. His thought is not merely “meditation philosophy.” It is a disciplined account of how the whole field of living becomes practice.

A decisive episode in his Chinese journey, often emphasized in interpretations of Dōgen, concerns his encounter with monastery cooks and the realization that mundane tasks are not inferior to formal meditation. Cooking, washing, arranging, cleaning, and serving can be complete practice. This is not moral decoration; it is central to the philosophy of mind. A mind is not merely what entertains representations. A mind is how a life is enacted in the kitchen, the monastery, the body, the word, the gesture, the moment. Dōgen’s later texts repeatedly extend practice beyond seated meditation into ordinary activities, while refusing to reduce zazen to a technique for producing special mental states.

This is why Dōgen’s position in the global history of philosophy of mind is unusual. He is earlier than Descartes by more than three centuries, and he does not inherit the same Greek-Christian-scholastic path that produced the modern European mind-body problem. Yet he addresses many of the same deep issues: selfhood, body, agency, perception, temporality, language, and the reality of experience. His answer is neither dualist nor materialist in the usual modern sense. It is practice-ontological.

4. Core Dōgenian concepts for a philosophy of mind

The most important concept is not “mind” alone but body-mind. Dōgen often speaks in terms of shinjin, body-mind, and the famous phrase associated with his awakening under Rujing is “dropping off body-mind.” This phrase should not be read as contempt for the body or as escape into a disembodied consciousness. It means the release of clinging to body and mind as self-owned, self-enclosed, objectifiable entities. Body-mind drops away when body-mind ceases to function as the imagined possession of an ego. In Dōgen’s own idiom, awakening is not a mind leaving the body; it is the de-reification of body-mind.

Another key concept is hishiryō, often rendered “nonthinking.” In Fukan zazengi, Dōgen instructs the practitioner not to pursue ordinary discursive calculation and not to sink into blankness. Nonthinking is not unconsciousness, trance, or the suppression of mental content. It is a mode of awareness not captured by discriminating, grasping, self-centered thought. A careful modern interpretation describes Dōgen’s “non-thinking” as a free flow of mental phenomena not seized and fixed into rigid categories, embedded in holistic bodily praxis rather than isolated intellectual operation.

A third concept is practice-realization, shushō ittō. Dōgen’s view is that practice and enlightenment are not sequentially separate. Official Sōtō presentations of this point rightly emphasize that practice itself is the goal of realization and that zazen is not a means to a reward lying outside it.

A fourth concept is genjōkōan, the actualization of the fundamental point. In the fascicle of that name, Dōgen’s most famous philosophical sequence says, in compressed form, that to study the Buddha way is to study the self, and to study the self is to forget the self. The self is then actualized by the myriad things. This is the heart of his philosophy of mind: self-knowledge culminates not in possession of an inner object but in self-forgetting, where the world itself verifies or actualizes the self.

A fifth concept is uji, being-time. In the fascicle Uji, Dōgen argues that time is not an external container through which beings pass. Being itself is temporal, and temporal moments are not detachable from beings. Mountains, oceans, persons, practice, language, and thought are not “in” time as objects inside a box. They are time. For philosophy of mind, this means that mental life is not an atemporal subject receiving temporal contents. Mind is itself temporal actualization.

A sixth concept is Buddha-nature. Dōgen transforms Buddha-nature away from any simple doctrine of an inner spiritual essence. He treats Buddha-nature dynamically and universally, extending sentience and realization language to mountains, rivers, grasses, trees, and the whole field of existence, while resisting the idea of a permanent metaphysical ground. Buddha-nature is not a hidden soul-substance; it is the dynamic suchness of things actualized in practice.

5. The ontology of mind: no substance, no private interior, no detached subject

Dōgen’s ontology of mind is anti-substantialist. The mind is not an immaterial substance, not a private Cartesian theater, not a metaphysical ego, and not a permanent witness. But neither is it simply an eliminable fiction. Mind is real as activity, relation, event, and practice.

The best formulation is: mind is body-mind-world functioning. This does not mean “mind is reducible to the body” in a crude physicalist sense. Nor does it mean “the world is reducible to mind” in a crude idealist sense. The contrast between mental interior and external object is itself part of deluded cognition when reified. Body, mind, and world are co-implicated in actual experience. The task is not to choose one substance as primary but to stop substantializing the field into separate, self-existing terms.

Dōgen’s Genjōkōan is decisive here. When the self advances toward things and confirms them, that is delusion; when the myriad things advance and confirm the self, that is awakening. Philosophically, this reverses the default representational picture. In the ordinary model, a subject stands over against objects and forms representations of them. In Dōgen’s awakened model, experience is not centered on an owner-subject. Things disclose, authenticate, and actualize the self.

This does not mean that Dōgen denies perspective. On the contrary, he has a subtle theory of finitude. The ocean may appear circular from one standpoint, but that appearance does not exhaust the ocean’s qualities. Fish and birds live in their respective elements, and their worlds are disclosed through embodied participation. One never occupies a God’s-eye view outside conditions. Mind is always situated, embodied, and perspectival; awakening does not abolish situatedness but releases the delusion that one’s current standpoint is an independent absolute.

This makes Dōgen surprisingly relevant to contemporary embodied and enactive approaches to mind. Embodied cognition challenges the Cartesian separation of mind from body and emphasizes cognition as interactive, world-involving, and manifested in bodily activity. Dōgen is not a contemporary cognitive scientist, but his body-mind-world model converges with the broad anti-Cartesian tendency of embodied approaches while retaining a distinctively Buddhist concern with delusion, awakening, and practice.

6. The self: self-study, self-forgetting, and world-actualization

Dōgen’s account of self is one of his deepest contributions to philosophy of mind. He does not begin by asking whether a self exists as a substance. He begins with the practical fact that we experience, act, cling, think, suffer, and seek. The self is therefore not dismissed; it is studied. But genuine self-study transforms the structure of self-relation. To study the self is to forget the self; to forget the self is to be actualized by the myriad things.

This is not nihilism. Dōgen is not saying that persons do not exist in any meaningful sense, or that ethical responsibility disappears. Rather, he denies the imagined self that stands behind body, thought, perception, and action as their owner. The self that is forgotten is the self as possessive center. What remains is not nothingness as blank absence, but a more intimate participation in the world.

Western comparison helps clarify the point. Hume famously argued that introspection does not reveal a substantial self, only a succession of perceptions; Kant, by contrast, argued that experience requires a unifying structure of apperception even if the self is not directly intuited as a substance. Dōgen shares with Hume the rejection of a directly apprehended self-substance, but he does not stop at skeptical bundle theory. He shares with Kant the sense that experience has a structure, but he does not ground that structure in a transcendental “I think.” Instead, Dōgen relocates selfhood in practice and world-actualization. The self is neither a metaphysical substance nor a mere heap of perceptions. It is an enacted relational event.

This is why “forgetting the self” is not self-annihilation. It is the transformation of selfing. The ordinary self is a contraction: it appropriates experience as “mine,” measures things by self-interest, and turns mind into an inner possession. The awakened self is not an owner but an openness through which things are allowed to be themselves. One might say that Dōgen replaces ego-subjectivity with responsive participation.

7. Body-mind: against dualism and against reductionism

Dōgen’s body-mind thought is neither Cartesian dualism nor reductive physicalism. Descartes’ classic dualism distinguishes mind and body as substances: mind is associated with thought, body with extension, and the modern mind-body problem arises in part from explaining their relation. Dōgen does not accept this starting point. Body and mind are not two separately existing substances that then need to be related. They are a single field of practice, experience, posture, action, and realization.

This is where Dōgen also differs from some forms of idealism. He does not dissolve the body into mind or treat the world as merely mental content. His constant attention to posture, breathing, eating, washing, robes, bowls, temple labor, and bodily discipline makes any disembodied reading untenable. But he also does not reduce mind to biological mechanism. His view is phenomenological and soteriological: body-mind is the lived field in which delusion and awakening are enacted.

A useful Western comparison is Aristotle. Aristotle’s psychology treats the soul as the form or actuality of a living body, not as a separable Cartesian substance. This hylomorphic framework avoids some later dualist problems because soul and body are not first posited as two independent things needing external connection. Dōgen converges with Aristotle in refusing a crude mind-body split. But the difference is equally important: Aristotle gives a biological-metaphysical account of soul as form of organism, whereas Dōgen gives a Buddhist-practical account of body-mind as impermanent, empty, and realized through practice.

“Dropping off body-mind” must be understood in this context. It is not the rejection of embodiment. It is the dropping of attachment to body and mind as fixed identity. The body still sits; the mind still thinks; the person still acts. But body and mind no longer appear as possessions of an independent ego. The duality that falls away is not the lived body but the reified body-mind complex.

8. Perception and intentionality: from representation to co-actualization

Western philosophy often defines intentionality as the “aboutness” or object-directedness of mental states. Dōgen’s account of perception is not framed as a theory of mental representation, but it can be reconstructed as a radical reconfiguration of intentionality. Ordinary intentionality is egocentric: the self advances toward things, categorizes them, confirms them, uses them, and appropriates them. Awakened intentionality is decentered: things come forth and actualize the self.

The key point is that perception is not a mirror relation between an inner subject and an outer object. Dōgen explicitly resists mirror-like models of experience. Seeing and hearing are carried out with fully engaged body-mind. This makes perception embodied, active, and world-involving.

His fish-and-water and bird-and-sky analogies are especially important. The fish does not first understand water from outside and then enter it; the bird does not master the sky as an object before flying. Each lives, moves, and realizes its field from within. So too with mind. Mind is not outside world, surveying it. Mind is always already in its element. But the element is not a static container; it is disclosed through activity.

This has direct relevance for contemporary debates about ecological and enactive cognition. Dōgen would reject a model in which the mind is a self-contained representational system that later maps an external environment. But he would also reject the opposite fantasy of an unconditioned, all-knowing, non-perspectival consciousness. Perception is embodied participation in a field whose full depth always exceeds the current standpoint.

9. Thought and nonthinking

Dōgen’s “nonthinking” is one of the most easily misunderstood aspects of his philosophy of mind. It does not mean the absence of thought, anti-intellectualism, blank trance, or mystical irrationality. Dōgen was a subtle writer who used paradox, philology, argument, quotation, and linguistic transformation with remarkable sophistication. Nonthinking is not stupidity. It is thought released from grasping.

In ordinary discriminative thinking, experience is divided, fixed, named, appropriated, and judged from the standpoint of self-concern. This is necessary for ordinary functioning, but it becomes delusive when treated as ultimate. Nonthinking allows thoughts to occur without being seized as “me,” “mine,” or “the final truth.” It is a mode of mind that neither suppresses cognition nor enthrones it.

The philosophical importance is substantial. Dōgen provides a model of consciousness in which mental activity can be present without being organized by reflective ego-possession. There can be awareness without a metaphysical subject, thought without attachment to thought, perception without representational domination, and agency without egoic authorship. This is not a theory of unconscious automaticity. It is disciplined, lucid, embodied awareness.

Dōgen’s account also resists the modern tendency to equate mind with explicit conceptualization. Mind includes posture, gesture, timing, silence, ritual, affective attunement, and non-discursive responsiveness. This makes it closer to skill, know-how, and embodied comportment than to detached theoretical representation.

10. Time and the mind: Dōgen’s being-time

The fascicle Uji, “being-time,” is indispensable for reconstructing Dōgen’s philosophy of mind. In many theories, time is treated as a container in which mental events occur. Dōgen rejects that picture. Being is time, and time is not separate from the beings that actualize it. The self is not an atemporal observer moving through a series of moments; the self is itself temporal. Practice, enlightenment, thought, speech, mountains, and oceans are all being-time.

This has several consequences for philosophy of mind. First, there is no timeless mental substance behind experience. Second, mental events are not merely located in time; they are temporal actualizations. Third, memory, anticipation, practice, and realization are not secondary additions to an already complete subject; they are ways the self is temporally configured. Fourth, enlightenment is not an escape from time but a liberated enactment of time.

Dōgen also resists the idea that time merely “flies away.” That ordinary image treats time as a line of vanishing now-points. Dōgen instead presents each moment as fully actual, relational, and expressive of the whole field of being-time. For mind, this means that a moment of awareness is not a thin instant owned by a subject. It is a complete event of world, body, practice, and realization.

Here Dōgen converges in interesting ways with later phenomenology, especially the analysis of temporality, embodiment, and world-disclosure. Phenomenology, as a Western movement, studies structures of consciousness from the first-person perspective and treats intentionality, perception, selfhood, embodiment, and temporality as central themes. But Dōgen differs from phenomenology in method and aim. He is not merely describing temporal consciousness; he is training body-mind to enact time without clinging.

11. Agency: action without an egoic controller

Dōgen’s philosophy of mind also implies a distinctive theory of agency. In ordinary thinking, agency is often imagined as an inner subject forming intentions and then causing bodily movements. Dōgen shifts the analysis. Action is not the output of a hidden controller; it is the full enactment of body-mind-world in a concrete situation.

The monastery cook, the seated practitioner, the person washing the face, the monk putting on robes, the practitioner bowing, the speaker speaking the Dharma: these are not merely bodies executing commands from an inner mind. They are sites of realization. Complete action does not require metaphysical ego-ownership. It requires total presence, responsiveness, and nonseparation of deed and doer.

This is why the unity of practice and realization matters for agency. If practice were merely a means to an external end, action would remain instrumental: the self does something now in order to get enlightenment later. Dōgen’s view dissolves this structure. In complete practice, the action is not a ladder to realization; the action is realization. Sōtō interpretations preserve this by emphasizing that the posture and practice of zazen are themselves realization, not steps toward a separate reward.

Philosophically, this is a theory of non-egoic agency. It avoids both libertarian ego-voluntarism and passive determinism. The practitioner acts, but the action is not owned by an isolated self. Agency becomes participatory, embodied, disciplined, and situated.

12. Language and mind

Dōgen is sometimes mistakenly associated with a crude Zen suspicion of language. In fact, he is one of the most linguistically inventive figures in Buddhist literature. He does not reject words; he rejects attachment to words as fixed representations. Language can delude when it freezes reality into conceptual idols, but language can also enact, disclose, and liberate.

This matters for philosophy of mind because it means Dōgen is not offering a simple opposition between conceptual thought and pure experience. He works through phrases, reversals, etymologies, inherited kōans, and grammatical disruptions. He uses language to loosen the mind’s attachment to ordinary conceptual oppositions: practice/enlightenment, self/world, body/mind, being/time, delusion/awakening.

A Dōgenian theory of language would say that words are not merely labels attached to pre-given objects by an inner subject. Words are events in being-time. Speech can be practice. A phrase can turn the body-mind. A kōan is not a puzzle to be solved by private cognition but an enacted situation in which the practitioner’s whole mode of understanding is tested.

This is another reason Dōgen cannot be reduced to ineffability mysticism. The point is not that reality is simply beyond language. The point is that language must be released from representational fixation and returned to practice.

13. Buddha-nature and the question of panpsychism

Dōgen’s Buddha-nature thought is essential but dangerous to oversimplify. When he speaks in ways that extend realization to mountains, rivers, grasses, trees, and the whole world, it may be tempting to call him a panpsychist. But that is at best a partial and potentially misleading comparison.

Panpsychism, in modern philosophy of mind, usually means that mind or experience is a basic feature of matter or reality. Dōgen is doing something different. He is not positing tiny minds inside all things. Nor is he saying that the universe is made of mental stuff. His Buddha-nature is dynamic, empty, and practice-realizational. It refuses the idea that Buddha-nature is a permanent hidden essence inside beings.

The better interpretation is that Dōgen de-centers the human mind. The human subject is not the sole site of meaning, reality, or disclosure. Mountains preach, rivers realize, walls and tiles express Dharma—not because they contain miniature human consciousnesses, but because the field of reality is not reducible to human representation. Awakening is not the human mind projecting meaning onto mute matter. It is the self being actualized by the myriad things.

Thus Dōgen’s view can converse with panpsychism, animism, ecological phenomenology, and new materialism, but it should not be identified too quickly with any of them. His position is neither “everything has a mind” nor “only human minds matter.” It is closer to: the human distinction between mind and world is itself a delusive abstraction when taken as ultimate.

14. Delusion as a philosophy-of-mind problem

For Dōgen, delusion is not simply having false beliefs. It is a whole structure of subject-centered experience. Delusion is the self’s attempt to secure itself as the one who knows, owns, practices, realizes, and controls. It is not only cognitive error; it is affective, bodily, temporal, linguistic, and practical.

This makes Dōgen’s account more comprehensive than many narrow theories of mind. A belief can be propositionally correct and still be delusively held. One may correctly state “all things are empty” and still cling to emptiness as a concept. One may know Buddhist doctrine and still enact self-centered grasping. Therefore, the transformation of mind cannot be achieved by information alone.

Awakening is correspondingly not mere belief revision. It is a transformation in how body-mind-world is actualized. The practitioner does not acquire an inner mental object called enlightenment. Rather, the possessive structure of acquisition drops away. Practice becomes the verification of what was never absent, yet never realized apart from practice.

15. Dōgen and Western philosophy of mind: major comparisons

The most obvious contrast is with Descartes. Cartesian dualism makes mind and body distinct substances and thereby sets up the modern mind-body problem as a problem of relation between thought and extension. Dōgen never accepts this ontological split. His “body-mind” is not two substances, and “dropping off body-mind” is not the liberation of a mental soul from a physical prison.

With Aristotle, Dōgen shares an anti-Cartesian refusal to treat soul and body as two independent entities. Aristotle’s hylomorphism treats soul as the actuality or form of a living body, which avoids some versions of the mind-body unity problem. But Aristotle remains within a framework of substance, form, organism, and teleological nature. Dōgen’s framework is emptiness, impermanence, no-self, and practice-realization.

With Hume, Dōgen shares the critique of a directly apprehensible self-substance. Hume’s introspective analysis finds perceptions rather than a stable self. But Dōgen is not merely skeptical or empiricist. He does not leave us with a bundle of impressions. He gives a disciplined path in which self-forgetting becomes world-actualization.

With Kant, Dōgen shares the sense that experience is not a passive reception of isolated data. Kant emphasizes synthesis, unity of experience, and transcendental apperception. Dōgen, however, does not ground experience in a transcendental subject. He is much more radical: the “I” that would unify experience is itself something to be studied, forgotten, and actualized by the myriad things.

With Hegel and German idealism, one can find suggestive parallels in the overcoming of rigid oppositions and in the idea that reality is not adequately understood through static atomism. Hegel is a post-Kantian systematic thinker concerned with dialectical development, history, and spirit. Dōgen is not a Hegelian. He does not present history as the self-development of Absolute Spirit. His nonduality is not a grand historical synthesis but the immediate, practice-realized actualization of each thing-time.

With phenomenology, the convergences are strong. Phenomenology studies first-person structures of consciousness, intentionality, perception, action, embodiment, temporality, selfhood, and world-experience. Dōgen’s thought also attends to lived experience, embodiment, temporality, and world-disclosure. Yet Dōgen is not merely descriptive. His phenomenology, if we may call it that, is transformative and normative. It asks not only “How does experience appear?” but “How does experience become delusion or awakening?”

With Heidegger, the comparison is especially tempting because of Dōgen’s being-time. Both reject the idea that being is a static object and both link existence to temporality. But Dōgen does not build an existential analytic of Dasein, nor does he privilege human being as the site from which Being is disclosed in the same way. His framework is Buddhist: no-self, dependent arising, Buddha-nature, zazen, and practice-realization.

With Merleau-Ponty and contemporary embodied cognition, Dōgen again shows striking convergence. Embodied cognition resists the image of a hidden, detached mind and emphasizes embodied, embedded, interactive cognition. Dōgen’s practice of zazen and his attention to bodily comportment make him a profound premodern resource for embodied accounts of mind. But he is more radical than many secular embodied theories because he treats embodiment not just as a condition for cognition but as the field of liberation.

With contemporary analytic philosophy of mind, Dōgen is both relevant and disruptive. He does not provide a standard theory of qualia, functional states, mental causation, supervenience, or neural correlates. But he challenges the assumptions that often generate those problems: that mind is an inner domain, that selfhood requires ownership by a subject, that perception is primarily representational, and that consciousness can be understood apart from practice, embodiment, temporality, and ethical transformation.

16. A systematic Dōgenian philosophy of mind: twelve theses

The first thesis is the no-owner thesis. Mental life does not belong to a permanent, independent self. Thoughts, perceptions, sensations, memories, and intentions occur, but the owner imagined behind them is a construction. This does not abolish persons; it abolishes metaphysical ego-possession.

The second thesis is the body-mind thesis. Mind is not separable from bodily posture, breath, gesture, affect, action, and environment. Body and mind are not two substances but one enacted field.

The third thesis is the world-involving thesis. Mind is not enclosed interiority. The self is actualized by the myriad things. Experience is world-involving from the beginning.

The fourth thesis is the practice-realization thesis. The mind is not transformed primarily by acquiring propositions about enlightenment. It is transformed by practice that is itself realization. Means and end are nondual.

The fifth thesis is the temporal-event thesis. Mental life is being-time. Thoughts, perceptions, words, and actions are not merely in time; they are temporal actualizations.

The sixth thesis is the perspectival-finitude thesis. Every disclosure occurs from within a standpoint, like the fish in water or the bird in sky. Awakening does not produce an abstract view from nowhere. It liberates situated experience from self-centered fixation.

The seventh thesis is the nonrepresentational correction. Ordinary cognition tends to treat the self as subject and the world as represented object. Dōgen reverses this: awakening is the myriad things advancing and actualizing the self.

The eighth thesis is the nonthinking thesis. The highest form of awareness is not blankness and not ordinary conceptual calculation. It is lucid, embodied, non-grasping thought beyond attachment to thought.

The ninth thesis is the anti-reification thesis. “Mind,” “body,” “self,” “time,” “Buddha-nature,” and “enlightenment” become delusive when grasped as fixed things. Dōgen’s method repeatedly releases concepts from substantialization.

The tenth thesis is the ethical-conduct thesis. Mind is not morally neutral inner processing. How one cooks, speaks, bows, cleans, sits, and responds is the actual shape of mind. Cognition, ethics, and practice are inseparable.

The eleventh thesis is the linguistic-enactive thesis. Language is not merely representation. Words can enact realization, disrupt fixation, and transform body-mind. But language also deludes when reified.

The twelfth thesis is the dynamic Buddha-nature thesis. Buddha-nature is not a private essence inside the mind. It is the dynamic, empty, all-inclusive actualization of reality through practice.

17. What Dōgen contributes to the philosophy of mind

Dōgen’s first contribution is a critique of the inner theater model. He gives us a way to think about consciousness without imagining a private spectator inside the head.

His second contribution is a non-nihilistic no-self theory. He denies the substantial ego without denying experience, responsibility, agency, or practice.

His third contribution is a body-mind account that avoids both substance dualism and crude reductionism. The body is not a machine controlled by mind; the mind is not a ghost inside the body; body-mind is enacted.

His fourth contribution is an enactive account of perception. Experience is not primarily the self’s representation of things; it is the co-actualization of self and world.

His fifth contribution is a temporal ontology of mental life. Mind is not an atemporal container of contents. It is being-time.

His sixth contribution is a theory of contemplative cognition. Nonthinking is a disciplined mode of awareness irreducible to both conceptual thought and blank absence.

His seventh contribution is an ethical expansion of philosophy of mind. Mind is not just what happens internally; it is how one lives. Conduct is not downstream from cognition. Conduct is cognition’s actual form.

His eighth contribution is methodological. Dōgen shows that philosophy of mind may require transformation of the philosopher. Some structures of mind are not adequately known by detached observation alone; they become intelligible through disciplined practice.

18. Misreadings to avoid

The first misreading is to make Dōgen a Cartesian mystic. He is not saying that a pure mind must escape the body. “Dropping off body-mind” is not disembodiment.

The second misreading is to make him a subjective idealist. He does not reduce mountains, rivers, and the world to mental contents.

The third misreading is to make him a simple materialist. He does not reduce mind to physiological mechanism or treat awakening as a brain state.

The fourth misreading is to make him a panpsychist in a straightforward modern sense. His Buddha-nature thought is not the thesis that every object contains a little consciousness.

The fifth misreading is to psychologize zazen as relaxation, attention training, or therapeutic self-regulation. It may have psychological effects, but Dōgen’s claim is ontological and soteriological: zazen is practice-realization.

The sixth misreading is to detach his philosophy from monastic conduct. For Dōgen, mind is revealed in practice. A purely theoretical Dōgen misses the point.

19. Dōgen’s later philosophical reception

Dōgen’s modern philosophical significance is partly a product of later rediscovery. His Shōbōgenzō was not always widely circulated, and broader philosophical engagement with it became especially important in modern Japan. Later Japanese thinkers, including figures associated with or adjacent to the Kyoto School, helped bring Dōgen into conversation with Western philosophy. Watsuji Tetsurō, for example, wrote on Dōgen and helped draw attention to Japanese philosophical sources, while the Kyoto School more broadly developed philosophical projects drawing from East Asian Mahāyāna Buddhism and Western thought.

This reception history matters because the very phrase “Dōgen’s philosophy of mind” is a modern reconstruction. Dōgen himself would not have separated philosophy of mind from ontology, ethics, language, practice, and liberation. The modern comparative task is therefore legitimate but delicate. We can place Dōgen in dialogue with Aristotle, Descartes, Hume, Kant, Hegel, phenomenology, and embodied cognition, but we should not force him into any one Western taxonomy.

20. Final synthesis

Dōgen’s philosophy of mind can be summarized in one compressed formula: mind is the nondual, temporal, embodied, world-involving activity of practice-realization, ordinarily misconstrued as an ego’s inner possession and awakened as self-forgetting actualized by the myriad things.

This gives us a powerful alternative to dominant Western starting points. Against Descartes, Dōgen denies that mind and body are separate substances. Against reductive materialism, he denies that the meaning of mind is exhausted by objective mechanism. Against subjective idealism, he denies that the world is merely mental content. Against representationalism, he treats perception as embodied co-actualization. Against ego theories of selfhood, he offers self-study that culminates in self-forgetting. Against atemporal accounts of consciousness, he gives being-time. Against purely theoretical philosophy, he insists that the mind is known and transformed in practice.

The result is not merely a Buddhist “answer” to the Western mind-body problem. It is a different way of framing the field. For Dōgen, the fundamental issue is not how an inner mind corresponds to an outer world. The fundamental issue is how the dualistic fixation on inner and outer, subject and object, practice and goal, self and world, body and mind, being and time, is enacted—and how it drops away.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
